subject: Neurology
question: A 28-year-old woman presents with unilateral throbbing headache associated with nausea, vomiting, and photophobia. Before the headache, she experienced flashing zigzag lights in her right visual field for 20 minutes. Similar episodes occur monthly around her menses. Neurological examination is normal. What is the most appropriate acute treatment?

options.text: Sumatriptan 50mg PO at onset of headache
options.explanation: Triptans are first-line for moderate-severe migraine. They are 5-HT1B/1D agonists that inhibit neurogenic inflammation and vasoconstriction. Best taken within 1 hour of headache onset. Contraindicated in cardiovascular disease.
Propranolol is for migraine PROPHYLAXIS (prevention), not acute treatment. Prophylaxis indicated if >4 attacks/month or attacks are disabling. This patient needs acute treatment for current attack.

NSAIDs are effective for MILD migraine but less effective for moderate-severe migraine with aura. Triptans are first-line for moderate-severe attacks. NSAIDs can be used as adjunct or for mild attacks.

Neuroimaging is not indicated for typical migraine with normal neurological exam. Imaging reserved for atypical features: thunderclap onset, focal deficits, or change in pattern. This is classic migraine with aura.
High-flow oxygen is first-line for CLUSTER headache, not migraine. Cluster headache presents with unilateral autonomic symptoms (lacrimation, rhinorrhea, Horner syndrome) and restlessness.

educationalObjective: Differentiate migraine from other headache disorders and select appropriate acute treatment (triptans first-line for moderate-severe attacks).
keyTakeaway: Triptans are first-line acute treatment for moderate-severe migraine. Take early in attack. Prophylaxis (beta-blockers, topiramate, CGRP antagonists) if >4 attacks/month.
